featuredQuotes.text: I think there's a lot of research showing that computers of any kind are a distraction in class and lead to cognitive overload. I'm a college professor and anecdotally I see major deficits in attention, critical and creative thinking, as well as basic social interaction. I ban all devices in my own classes for this reason.

my child's behavior and focus are directly impacted by screen time. Screens are addicting and pushing it instead of book for education does more harm than good in elementary schools. All screens should be removed from elementary schools. Patents should not have to pay 30k a year to send their kid to a waldorf school to not have the tech. This is crazy

These devices are a nightmare for kids with issues with attention span/executive function deficits. They are a massive source of distraction in school and out of school. Some kids are absolutely addicted to screens, and we should not be feeding that addiction at school.

- Kids are playing videogames, shopping, and exposed to inappropriate content during school hours. District Admin knows this - for over a year - and isn't stopping it. 

- For every 1 kid doing that, there are 5 around them who are horribly, unfairly distracted.

I’m very concerned about the lack of evidence of growth using ed tech vs “old school methods”. The data suggests their is no growth using the ed tech and could argue it is creating a disconnect between student and teacher/classroom. The more we rely on AI and Ed Tech, the more detrimental to the development of our children.

The large Smart Screen covers the whiteboard area, and when it's on, I see that it dominates the attention of the young children. Often during drop off in the morning there is a song playing on Youtube - with kids focused on the screen as they dance (or not). They often watch movies during rest time after lunch.

The school trys to put restritions on the computer so the kids can't play games at school, on the bus and at home but the students always find away around it.  I have ways of regulating their screen time on the devices I give them, but it's impossible to regulate it well when they have they school issued device at home all the time.

My understanding is that next year (3rd grade) the Chromebooks start coming home and usage really ramps up. This is entirely unacceptable to me. I don’t mind its limited use as one of many tools in school, but I think inviting screens in to a greater extent opens up all of the issues above, plus the potential for cyber bullying.
